invariant_definition: *Karaṇa* is the instrumental role within Pāṇinian *kāraka*-analysis: *sādhakatamaṃ karaṇam* (Aṣṭādhyāyī 1.4.42) — that which is most-instrumental to the accomplishment of the action. Beyond grammar, the term has at least three further technical lives across the Indian schools. (1) In Sāṃkhya, the *trayodaśa-karaṇa* (thirteen-instrument) doctrine analyzes the cognitive apparatus — *buddhi*, *ahaṅkāra*, *manas*, the five *jñānendriya*s, the five *karmendriya*s — as the *karaṇa*-set through which *puruṣa*'s experience and action are mediated; the *antaḥkaraṇa* is *karaṇa* internal, the senses *karaṇa* external. (2) In Vedānta epistemology *karaṇa* names the operative cause of cognition (*pramā-karaṇa* = *pramāṇa*) — defining *pramāṇa* itself as the most-effective instrument of veridical cognition. (3) In Vaiśeṣika causal analysis *karaṇa* is one of three causal types: *samavāyi-kāraṇa* (inherent cause, e.g., the threads in cloth), *asamavāyi-kāraṇa* (non-inherent cause, e.g., the conjunction of threads), *nimitta-kāraṇa* (efficient cause, e.g., the weaver). The unifying core is the relational sense: *karaṇa* names whatever stands as the indispensable means by which the *kartṛ*'s activity reaches its *kārya*. Vedāntic schools deploy the term across all three registers and inherit the Sāṃkhyan and Vaiśeṣikan technical apparatus while disagreeing on its ontological status (real *acit* vs. *upādhi* of *avidyā* vs. dependent *śakti*).

per_school.definition: In Advaita, `karaṇa` in the strict psychological sense is the `antaḥkaraṇa`, the upādhi through which `jīvatva` and the reflected appearance of consciousness are articulated in the exegesis of `tvam`. [[SS III.278](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/3.278)] The rise of liberating knowledge does not depend on ritual action or on the unaided commerce of the organs, since for the attainment of `jñāna` the Veda alone is acknowledged as `pramāṇa`, and the mahāvākya yields its force through `vākyārtha-pratipatti`. [[UpS I.17.7-9](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9)] All differentiated apparatus, including instrumental cognition, belongs only to the māyika display in which the unborn appears as manifold. [[MK 3.19-21](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prakaraṇa.19-21)]

The `ātman` is distinct from body, senses, mind, vital breaths, and cognitions, so the `karaṇa`-complex belongs to the embodied self as a dependent apparatus rather than to the self's own `svarūpa`. [[ĀS 3](cite://yamuna/atma-siddhi/3)] Vedāntic revelation is directed to manifesting the self as body-transcendent and to disclosing the `Paramātman` as the `antaryāmin` for whom the self itself stands in the `śarīra`-relation. [[VS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)] Because the self's essential nature is `jñānānandaika-guṇa`, the sensory and mental instruments remain karma-conditioned embodiments rather than the substance of consciousness. [[VS §5](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5)]

The school's `prāmāṇya` analysis defines `svatastva` as origination dependent solely on the producer of the cognition, so epistemic inquiry begins by isolating a determinate operative source. [[Prāmāṇyavāda, utpatti-svatastva-lakṣaṇa](cite://vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/Prāmāṇyavāda, utpatti-svatastva-lakṣaṇa)] In the proof of `svato-grāhyatva`, the example `rūpaṃ cakṣurgrāhyam` treats the eye as a distinct `karaṇa` whose object is grasped through its own proper function. [[TT 1, Prāmāṇyavāda, svato-grāhyatvānumāna](cite://vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/1.prāmāṇyavāda)] The sequence closes by affirming that the unauthored Veda is indeed `pramāṇa`, extending the school's account of operative instrumentality from the senses to authoritative verbal cognition. [[Prāmāṇyavāda conclusion](cite://vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/conclusion)]

`Karaṇa` appears as one member of the conventional nexus `kārya-kāraṇa-kartṛ-karaṇa-kriyā` invoked in the objection that emptiness would overturn all intelligible practice. [[MMK 24.17](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.17)] Nāgārjuna's reply is that precisely this dependently arisen nexus is `śūnyatā`, a `prajñapti` grounded in relational uptake, and that is the middle way. [[MMK 24.18](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18)] Because the ultimate is taught only by relying on `vyavahāra`, instrumentality is retained as worldly convention while denied any `svabhāva`. [[MMK 24.10](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.10)]

translator_note: 'Instrument' is the standard and acceptable gloss, but it can mislead in two ways. First, in Sāṃkhya the *karaṇa*-set includes the *buddhi* and *manas* — not just sense-organs — so 'instrument' must include cognitive faculties. Second, in Vaiśeṣika causal analysis *karaṇa* often picks out the *operative* (efficient) cause specifically, not 'instrument' in the loose sense. The most damaging error is to confuse *karaṇa* with *kāraṇa* (कारण), 'cause' — these are distinct technical terms, though related etymologically: *karaṇa* (नपुं) = instrument-role; *kāraṇa* (नपुं) = cause.
